title: Drosophila Egg Dechorionation
author: Katy M Monteith
license_note: Confirmed via CrossRef DOI metadata: dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.vcze2x6

steps.title: 50 mL falcon tube with the bottom ~2 inches removed
steps.instruction: 50 mL falcon tube with the bottom ~2 inches removed. Fig

steps.title: 50 mL falcon tube lid with...
steps.instruction: 50 mL falcon tube lid with the centre of the lid top removed. Fig

steps.title: Ready to use egg dechorionator
steps.instruction: Ready to use egg dechorionator. Gauze fabric is screwed into the lid providing a mesh barrier to collect the eggs.

steps.title: Prepare apple agar petri dishes Recipe...
steps.instruction: Prepare apple agar petri dishes Recipe for apple-agar embryo collection plates by Katy M Monteith

steps.title: Set up egg cage with appropriate...
steps.instruction: Set up egg cage with appropriate flies as described in the Drosophila embryo collection protocol. Carry out steps 1-4, so that your final step is removing any debris/dead flies from the petri dish using the soft tweezers. Drosophila embryo collection by Katy M Monteith

steps.title: Pour triple distilled water into the...
steps.instruction: Pour triple distilled water into the petri dish until the agar is fully submerged. Carefully use the paintbrush to dislodge the eggs from the agar surface

steps.title: Once all the eggs are dislodged,...
steps.instruction: Once all the eggs are dislodged, pour the water/egg solution from the petri dish into the egg dechorionator placed in an empty beaker- allowing the eggs to collect on the gauze and the water to flow through into the beaker

steps.title: Repeat steps 4 & 5 if...
steps.instruction: Repeat steps 4 & 5 if eggs still remain on the agar surface

steps.title: Place 2x 250ml beakers (one containing...
steps.instruction: Place 2x 250ml beakers (one containing ~90 mL of 7-10% bleach and one containing ~90 mL of triple distilled water), hard tweezers, paintbrush, container of 7-10% bleach, container of triple distilled water, timer and premade autoclaved food tubes into a laminar flow hood- see guidelines.

steps.title: Using the hard tweezers, pick up...
steps.instruction: Using the hard tweezers, pick up the egg dechorionator and place immediately into the beaker containing bleach within the flow hood.

steps.title: Gently and periodically move the egg...
steps.instruction: Gently and periodically move the egg dechorionator up and down in the bleach for 2min 30sec using the hard tweezers, ensuring the eggs are submerged in the bleach.

steps.title: Discard the bleach, add another 90...
steps.instruction: Discard the bleach, add another 90 mL of 7-10% bleach to beaker and repeat step 9.

steps.title: Move the egg dechorionator from the...
steps.instruction: Move the egg dechorionator from the bleach directly into the beaker containing triple distilled water, rinse in the distilled water for ~30 seconds by gently and periodically moving up and down. Discard the water and repeat a further two times until the eggs have been completely rinsed of bleach solution.

steps.title: The eggs should now be dechorionated and ready to use
steps.instruction: The eggs should now be dechorionated and ready to use. If rearing the resulting offspring, immediately move the eggs from dechorionator to autoclaved food vials/bottles using a paintbrush. Alternatively, if density controlling is required, re-suspend the eggs in triple distilled water in a falcon tube and squirt into food vials/bottles following steps 6-8 of the Drosophila embryo collection protocol (see step 3.6-3.8 above). For both options, perform within a laminar flow hood with sterile equipment. It is possible to check if the eggs have been successfully dechorionated by inspection under a microscope prior to adding to food media. Eggs will look like smooth prolonged ovoids lacking the characteristic dorsal appendages.
